title: St. John the Baptist, St. Narcissus of Jerusalem, St. Pandoleon & St. Silas the Apostle
narrative:
title": "St. John the Baptist, St. Narcissus of Jerusalem, St. Pandoleon & St. Silas the Apostle

narrative": "St. John the Baptist, St. Narcissus of Jerusalem, St. Pandoleon the Martyr, and St. Silas the Apostle

On the final day of the month of Yekatit, the holy Church closes the Yekatit cycle with four witnesses who together embody the themes that have animated the whole month: the prophetic voice, the elderly shepherd, the young martyr, and the faithful companion. Saint John the Baptist is commemorated on this thirtieth day at the Finding of his Sacred Head — that moment when the relics of the Forerunner, buried after his martyrdom at the command of Herodias, were discovered by the faithful and enshrined as a living presence in the Church. The Baptist's severed head, which had spoken truth to Herod and been silenced by the sword, is found and honoured by the Church as a witness to the truth that no act of power can permanently silence the voice of God. The head that proclaimed Behold the Lamb of God continues to proclaim it from the reliquary, from the icon, from the liturgical commemoration that carries his words into every age.

Saint Narcissus of Jerusalem is among the most remarkable episcopal figures of the early Church: appointed bishop of Jerusalem at an advanced age, he governed the holy city's community with wisdom and was then driven from his see by three false accusations of the most serious kind — his accusers swore their false testimony by calling down upon themselves death, disease, and fire if they were lying. All three deaths came precisely as sworn: one by fire, one by disease, one by a sudden and terrible end. Narcissus, vindicated but wearied by the ordeal, retreated to the desert for years of solitary prayer, during which the community tried three successors in his place, only for Narcissus to return at extreme old age, said to be over a hundred and ten, to serve his church once more alongside the young Alexander who would eventually succeed him. The Ethiopian tradition honours Narcissus as a sign that God vindicates the patient.

Saint Pandoleon is this day's figure of young martyrdom — a Christian of noble birth who refused the imperial command to sacrifice and was put to death with a courage that the tradition associates with those whose faith had not yet had time to grow complicated or cautious. The Synaxarium preserves him alongside the Baptist and Narcissus as a reminder that the Church's witness is not the property of the old and experienced alone: that the young, the untried, and the newly consecrated are as fully capable of the ultimate testimony as the aged prophet or the hundred-year-old bishop, because martyrdom is not a matter of experience but of grace, and grace does not consult the calendar.

Saint Silas the Apostle brings the month of Yekatit to its close with the figure of the faithful companion — the man who accompanied Paul through the second missionary journey, who sang hymns with him at midnight in the Philippian prison, who bore the marks of beating alongside him, and who continued the apostolic mission after their separation, becoming by tradition the first bishop of Corinth. Silas is one of those figures whose greatness lies entirely in relation — in the quality of his partnership, his willingness to be second, his constancy in circumstances that would have broken a man in it for himself. He and Onesimus and Titus and Archippus and the other Pauline companions form a community of secondary witnesses without whom the primary mission could not have been accomplished. The Baptist who prepared the way, Narcissus who waited in the desert, Pandoleon who died young, Silas who walked beside another: Yekatit ends as it began, with the company of the faithful closing ranks around the mystery they have been given to guard, each one indispensable, none of them seeking to be the greatest. Amen.
